assessor_1_raw: The retrospective evaluation is on a selected population (approved loans that matured). The proposed expansion specifically targets segments likely to have been excluded from this population. Strong performance on approved applicants does not imply strong performance on excluded applicants — this is a fundamental generalization problem in lending. A champion-challenger pilot is the standard industry approach to address this.
assessor_2_raw: AUROC measures ranking quality among observed borrowers. The gap between this and 'ready to automate approval decisions' involves: choosing a threshold, analyzing false positive rates (wrong approvals) at that threshold, projecting portfolio loss implications, and performing sensitivity analysis across economic conditions. None of this is in the memo. The claim that the model 'justifies broader automated approvals' is not supported by ranking metrics alone.
assessor_3_raw: Device signals and employment-text embeddings as credit decision inputs raise immediate fair lending questions: are these features disparately impacting protected classes? Can declines based on these features be explained in language that satisfies ECOA adverse action notice requirements? Has the model been tested for proxy discrimination? These are legal compliance questions that must be answered before any live deployment, regardless of model performance.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — scope_intent_credit_launch_303 — Ensemble Run 2

Consistent with Run 1. All three issues identified by all three assessors.

Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED

empirical_test.measure: champion-challenger pilot; realized 12-month default rates; disparate impact analysis; adverse action explainability assessment
empirical_test.success_criterion: calibration holds in expansion segments; regulatory requirements satisfied
empirical_test.failure_criterion: calibration degrades; disparate impact found; regulatory requirements infeasible with current feature set
